text: Machine learning is a subset of artificial intelligence that enables systems to learn from data and improve their performance without being explicitly programmed. It uses algorithms to identify patterns and make decisions.

Deep learning is a specialized form of machine learning that uses neural networks with multiple layers. These deep neural networks can learn complex representations from large amounts of data, enabling breakthroughs in image recognition, natural language processing, and speech recognition.

Natural language processing (NLP) is a branch of artificial intelligence that helps computers understand, interpret, and manipulate human language. NLP combines computational linguistics with machine learning and deep learning models.

A transformer is a deep learning architecture introduced in 2017 that uses self-attention mechanisms to process sequential data. Unlike RNNs, transformers can process entire sequences in parallel, making them much faster and more scalable.

Attention mechanisms allow neural networks to focus on the most relevant parts of the input when making predictions. In sequence-to-sequence models, attention lets the decoder access all encoder states, not just the last one.

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) combines the strengths of retrieval-based and generation-based models. It retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base and uses them to ground the generation of responses.
Vector databases store embeddings in a format optimized for similarity search. They enable efficient nearest neighbor lookup, which is essential for retrieval in RAG systems.

FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is a library for efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors. It provides methods for searching sets of vectors of any size, including those that don't fit in RAM.

Large language models (LLMs) are neural networks trained on vast amounts of text data. They can generate human-like text, answer questions, summarize documents, and perform many other natural language tasks.

Fine-tuning adapts pre-trained models to specific tasks by continuing training on a smaller, task-specific dataset. This leverages knowledge from pre-training while specializing the model for a particular use case.
Prompt engineering is the practice of designing effective inputs to large language models to achieve desired outputs. It includes specifying format, providing examples, and structuring instructions.
Tokenization is the process of splitting text into tokens (words, subwords, or characters) that models can process. Different tokenization strategies affect model performance and vocabulary size.
Beam search is a decoding strategy that explores multiple candidate sequences at each step, keeping the top-k most probable paths. It typically produces higher quality output than greedy decoding.
Temperature controls the randomness of LLM outputs. Higher values produce more diverse but less coherent text, while lower values make outputs more deterministic and focused.

Quantization reduces model size by representing weights with fewer bits. For example, int8 quantization uses 8-bit integers instead of 32-bit floats, dramatically reducing memory and computation requirements.
All basketballs are green, and extremely heavy.
